ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general button mushroom food product entry; the sauteed preparation is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked mushrooms broadly; no specific sauteed button mushroom sub-code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sauteed button mushroom. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of sauteed button mushrooms is approximately 80 g, equivalent to about 6–8 medium mushrooms cooked in a small amount of butter or oil.

nutrient_source.title: Button mushroom, sauteed — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14020002 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken as-is from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at approximately 1.0 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for mushrooms, white, cooked, sauteed (FDC ID 169251), which reports ~0.9 g saturated fat per 100 g for butter-sauteed preparation. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat values are set to -1 as HPB does not report them for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for sauteed button mushroom exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Mushrooms are a very low-carbohydrate vegetable (5.36 g carbohydrate/100 g, of which 2.26 g is fibre, yielding ~3.1 g available carbohydrate). GI is estimated at 15 based on the closest analogous entry for cooked non-starchy vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (15 × 3.1 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 0.37, rounded to 0.6 using full carbohydrate basis (5.36 × 0.80 × 15 / 100 = 0.64).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and high potassium (339 mg/100 g) yielding a strongly favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Sauteed button mushrooms are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals; their very low sodium content and high potassium content actively support blood pressure regulation, provided no additional salt is added during cooking and butter use is moderated to limit saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (~3.1 g/100 g net), high dietary fibre (2.26 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~15) result in minimal postprandial glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sauteed button mushrooms are suitable for regular inclusion in a type 2 diabetic diet due to their very low glycemic load, high fibre content that slows gastric emptying, and negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose; portion size of oil or butter used in sauteing should be monitored to avoid excess caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1 g/100 g), no trans fat, high potassium (339 mg/100 g), and beta-glucan content from mushrooms support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sauteed button mushrooms present a low cardiovascular risk profile; using extra-virgin olive oil instead of butter for sauteing further reduces saturated fat intake and adds oleic acid and polyphenol benefits, making this preparation a heart-healthy side dish when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucans in button mushrooms modulate immune response; low saturated fat and absence of trans fat reduce pro-inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sauteed button mushrooms are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; sauteing in extra-virgin olive oil adds oleocanthal, a natural COX inhibitor with anti-inflammatory properties, and excessive salt addition should be avoided to minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Button Mushroom (Sauteed) consists of white button mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus) cooked in butter or vegetable oil over medium-to-high heat until golden brown, typically seasoned with garlic, salt, and fresh or dried herbs such as thyme or parsley. The sauteing process concentrates the mushrooms' umami flavour, producing a tender yet slightly firm texture with caramelised edges. Per 100 g, this preparation provides 78 kcal, 2.15 g protein, 6.06 g fat (predominantly from the cooking fat), 5.36 g carbohydrate, 2.26 g dietary fibre, and only 5 mg sodium, making it one of the lowest-sodium cooked vegetable preparations available. Potassium is notably high at 339 mg/100 g, supporting a potassium-to-sodium ratio strongly aligned with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 80 g serving is under 1, making this food clinically suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Button mushrooms are a source of beta-glucans, which have immunomodulatory properties relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free; it is not vegan when prepared with butter. It is widely consumed as a side dish, breakfast accompaniment, or topping in Western, fusion, and contemporary Singaporean cuisine contexts.
common_user_queries: Are sauteed mushrooms good for diabetics?
How many calories in sauteed button mushrooms?
Can I eat sauteed mushrooms if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of cooked mushrooms?
How much potassium in sauteed mushrooms?
Is sauteed mushroom good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14020002, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169251 for sauteed white mushrooms as HPB does not report this field. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked non-starchy vegetables, clearly documented. All other fields are derived from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for F14020002 (Button mushroom, sauteed) and are not available in the HPB database for this specific preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 1.0 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169251 (mushrooms, white, cooked, sauteed) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for cooked non-starchy vegetables per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for sauteed button mushroom was found in approved sources. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false as the standard preparation uses butter; a vegan variant using plant-based oil would qualify as vegan. is_kosher set to false as mixing butter with potential meat accompaniments raises kashrut concerns; mushroom alone is pareve. Allergen note: milk allergen applies only when butter is used; oil-based preparation is dairy-free.
